content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1160 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

January 9, 1978

James L. Smith
Department Auditor
The American Legion
War Memorial Veterans Building
San Francisco, CA 94102

Dear Mr. Smith:

Thank you for your letter of December 21, 1977.

As you may be aware, in its decision in the case of Buckley
v. Valeo, 424 U.S. 1 (1976), the United States Supreme Court
required that minor parties and candidates be granted ex-
emptions from campaign contribution disclosure if a proper
showing of threats and harassment to contributors were made.
The Commission had filed a friend of the court brief in the
Buckley case urging that no exemptions be permitted.

Once the Buckley case was decided, the Commission
believed that it was necessary, as a matter of constitutional
law as set out in Buckley, to provide a means for determining
whether certain minor parties were entitled to exemptions.
This exemption procedure was created and as your organiza-
tion's resolution notes, several persons were examined ex
emptions. However, during 1977 the Legislature passed and
the Governor signed a bill which prohibits the Commission
from granting exemptions from the campaign disclosure re-
quirements of the Political Reform Act. That bill became
effective January 1, 1978, and is contained in Section 84400
of the Government Code. The Commission intends to abide by
the legislative will expressed in Section 84400 and will not
accept applications for exemptions nor grant any exemptions.

Despite the fact that the Commission no longer may
grant exemptions, your [illegible] and affiliates still will be

James L. Smith
Page Two
January 9, 1978


entitled to exemptions if they meet the criteria set out in
Buckley and will seek such exemptions from the courts.

If I can be of any further assistance in this
matter, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Lee C. Rosenthal
Acting Chief
Legal Division

LCR:jo

December 21, 1977


Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Chairman Lowenstein:

Attached is a copy of Department of California,
The American Legion Resolution 77-501, which was
approved unanimously by the delegates to the
59th Annual American Legion State Convention held
in Palm Springs in June of 1977.


Sincerely,


James L. Smith
Department Adjutant


JLS:f
Att.

THE AMERICAN LEGION
DEPARTMENT OF CALIFORNIA
117 WAR MEMORIAL VETERANS BLDG.
SAN FRANCISCO 94102


Res. 77-501    Oppose Permanent Exemption to Communists and
               Socialists Under the State Fair Political
               Practice Commission


WHEREAS, California's campaign contribution law requires that
the identity of contributors of $50.00 or more be
disclosed, and

WHEREAS, the State Fair Political Practice Commission has seen
fit to grant temporary exemptions to two Socialists and
one Communist party candidates, and

WHEREAS, the disclosure law is a key element of a major political
reform package approved by the voters in 1974, now,
therefore, be it

RESOLVED by The American Legion, Department in regular convention
assembled at Palm Springs, California, June 23-26, 1977,
that The American Legion is deeply concerned, that
Communist and Socialists are to be granted exemptions which
are not available to traditional political parties, and, be
it further

RESOLVED that this concern be made known to the Fair Political Practice
Commission, and that we strongly urge that no permanent exemption
be granted to any one, and that it be further

RESOLVED that these facts be made known to the Fair Political Practice
Commission, by the Department, in the most effective way possible.


THE ABOVE RESOLUTION WAS ADOPTED BY THE AMERICAN LEGION, DEPARTMENT
OF CALIFORNIA, IN REGULAR CONVENTION ASSEMBLED IN PALM SPRINGS,
CALIFORNIA, JUNE 23-26, 1977.

sections.question_synthetic: What is the FPPC's policy regarding exemptions from campaign contributor disclosure requirements for minor parties and candidates, particularly in light of the American Legion's concerns about exemptions granted to communist and socialist party candidates?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC will no longer grant exemptions from campaign disclosure requirements as of January 1, 1978, pursuant to Section 84400 of the Government Code passed by the Legislature in 1977, though minor parties and candidates may still seek exemptions directly from courts if they meet the Buckley v. Valeo criteria.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of incoming request (American Legion resolution dated December 21, 1977) followed by FPPC response (dated January 9, 1978). No formal Q/C sections present; question and conclusion synthesized from context. Acting Chief Lee C. Rosenthal provides the response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What is the FPPC's policy regarding exemptions from campaign contributor disclosure requirements for minor parties and candidates, particularly in light of the American Legion's concerns about exemptions granted to communist and socialist party candidates?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC will no longer grant exemptions from campaign disclosure requirements as of January 1, 1978, pursuant to Section 84400 of the Government Code passed by the Legislature in 1977, though minor parties and candidates may still seek exemptions directly from courts if they meet the Buckley v. Valeo criteria.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to The American Legion's resolution opposing permanent exemptions for communist and socialist party candidates by explaining that a new state law (effective January 1, 1978) prohibits the FPPC from granting any exemptions from campaign disclosure requirements, though courts may still grant them based on constitutional grounds established in Buckley v. Valeo.
